title: Hormuz Talks Resume Under Ultimatum, Spain's Deadliest Wildfire in Decades, Typhoon Bavi Menaces East Asia
subhead: The US demands Iran publicly declare the Strait of Hormuz open and stop firing on commercial ships ahead of Saturday negotiations in Oman. A wildfire in southern Spain kills at least 12, with 23 missing. Typhoon Bavi — one of the largest storms in decades — bears down on Taiwan and southeastern China after killing 15 in the Philippines.

sections.heading: US Demands Hormuz Declaration as Talks Resume in Oman
sections.body:
The United States has told Iran it must publicly declare the Strait of Hormuz open to all commercial shipping and pledge to stop firing on vessels traversing the waterway, senior US officials told reporters on Friday. The ultimatum comes ahead of negotiations scheduled for Saturday in Oman, where Vice-President JD Vance, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, special envoy Steve Witkoff, and Jared Kushner will represent the US side.

US media reported that Iranian officials privately acknowledged to Trump's advisers that the attacks on commercial ships this week were a mistake, attributing them to a rogue internal hardline faction. One official told CBS: 'They came back to the table and said, we screwed up. We made a mistake. Let's keep talking.' The White House also wants Iran to publicly acknowledge that the shooting was an error, according to CBS.

Trump declared on Truth Social that 'the Cease Fire is OVER!' but confirmed both sides had agreed to continue talks. Qatar dispatched a delegation to Iran on Friday for parallel discussions on de-escalation. No fresh attacks on shipping were reported on Friday after three ships were struck earlier in the week on the US-recommended Omani route. Iran's Fars news agency reported that under the June 17 memorandum, the strait would ultimately be managed by Iran in coordination with Oman, including possible 'service fees' for transiting ships.
sections.whyItMatters: The MoU's collapse earlier this week and Trump's declaration that the ceasefire is 'over' left both sides without a functioning framework. The Saturday talks are a test of whether Iran can deliver on a public commitment to stop attacks — something it claims rogue elements violated. If Iran refuses the declaration, the US has signaled negotiations will not proceed, and the military escalation that saw 90 Iranian targets struck this week could resume.

sections.heading: Russia Escalates Attacks on Zaporizhzhia as Drone Tactics Shift
sections.body:
Russian attacks on the Ukrainian city of Zaporizhzhia have intensified dramatically, with drones and glide bombs hitting buses, schools, petrol stations, and government offices in recent weeks. The city of 750,000 people sits just 24km from the front line and is experiencing its worst security deterioration of the war, according to the BBC's reporting from the ground.

Sam Cranny-Evans of the Royal United Services Institute told the BBC that Russian forces are using longer-range 'mothership' drones that release several smaller FPV (first-person-view) drones to strike targets previously out of range. Russian forces are also deploying mesh networking technology that relays radio signals from drone to drone, making them harder to jam. Ukrainian authorities say they intercepted 884 Russian drones in the last week of June alone.

While Russian troops have been forced to retreat south of Zaporizhzhia, they continue advancing elsewhere at a slower pace. Ukraine has responded by escalating its own campaign: strikes on Russian oil refineries, fuel storage, and logistics in occupied territories have intensified. Russia has banned diesel exports, and over 90 percent of its regions are experiencing fuel rationing or shortages.
sections.whyItMatters: The shift to mothership drones and mesh networking represents a tactical evolution that extends Russian strike depth without requiring ground advances. Zaporizhzhia's experience — a major city under sustained drone bombardment just 24km from the front — shows how the war's character is changing even as the front line barely moves. Ukraine's parallel campaign against Russian fuel logistics is creating the kind of supply pressure that could affect military operations, but it has not yet translated into territorial gains.

sections.heading: Spain Suffers One of Its Deadliest Wildfires as Climate Shock Intensifies
sections.body:
At least 12 people have been killed and 23 remain missing in a wildfire near the village of Bédar in Almería, southern Spain, in what Andalusia's emergency minister Antonio Sanz called 'the most devastating fire' the region has ever seen. Four of the victims are believed to be British nationals found trapped in their car. Another four people are hospitalized with serious burns.

Andalusia's regional leader Juanma Moreno said the fire appeared to have been caused by a downed power line, though electricity company Endesa contested this, claiming the fallen line was inactive and did not belong to them. Around 1,000 residents were evacuated and 220 soldiers deployed from Spain's Military Emergency Unit.

The disaster comes amid a sustained heatwave across Southern Europe with temperatures around 40°C (104°F). Last year, a record 393,000 hectares burned in Spain — more than six times the 2006-2024 average, according to the European Forest Fire Information System. The World Weather Attribution group at Imperial College London has directly linked worsening Mediterranean fire seasons to climate change. Europe is warming twice as fast as the global average, per the Copernicus climate service.
sections.whyItMatters: This fire is already among the deadliest in Spanish history, and the death toll may rise with 23 still missing. The debate over the downed power line — with the utility disputing responsibility — highlights the accountability gap in infrastructure maintenance as climate-driven fire risk escalates. With 393,000 hectares burned last year and extreme heat now routine, Southern Europe is facing a structural wildfire crisis that existing emergency response capacity is struggling to match.

sections.heading: Typhoon Bavi Heads for Taiwan and China After Killing 15 in the Philippines
sections.body:
Typhoon Bavi, spanning 1,000km at its widest point — roughly the width of France — is sweeping across the Pacific toward Taiwan and southeastern China after triggering landslides that killed at least 15 people on the Philippine island of Mindanao. Taiwan's Central Weather Administration said Bavi could be the largest storm by size to hit the island since 1987.

Taiwan has put 29,000 soldiers on standby and warned of up to 1 meter of rainfall. Dozens of flights have been cancelled across the region — Japan Airlines grounded over 100 flights affecting 20,000 passengers, All Nippon Airways cancelled more than 160, and Thai Airways and Malaysia Airlines suspended Taipei routes. China's meteorological authorities warned of 'significant impact' as Bavi is forecast to make landfall in Fujian province, with some models suggesting it could make a second landfall further north — in provinces with 'less experience' dealing with typhoons, according to environmental director Ma Jun of China's Institute of Public and Environmental Affairs.

Southern China is still recovering from Typhoon Maysak, which killed at least 39 people earlier this week, forced 130,000 evacuations, and spawned two rare tornadoes in Hubei province.
sections.whyItMatters: Bavi is hitting a region already strained by Maysak's aftermath, compounding damage to infrastructure, agriculture, and displacement. The potential for a double landfall in China raises the risk for northern provinces unused to typhoon response. Back-to-back major storms in the same week — one of them among the largest in decades — are consistent with the intensification pattern that climate scientists have linked to warmer ocean temperatures in the western Pacific.

sections.heading: Trump Empties Federal Election Commission Months Before Midterms
sections.body:
President Trump has removed the last remaining members of the Election Assistance Commission, the independent federal body created by Congress in 2002 to support state and local election officials. The two Democratic appointees — Thomas Hicks and Benjamin Hovland — were fired by email on Thursday, according to Reuters. The sole remaining Republican, Christy McCormick, resigned. A fourth commissioner, Donald Palmer, had already left in April.

The EAC is required by law to be composed of an equal number of Democrats and Republicans. It certifies voting systems, creates non-binding election guidelines, and maintains the national mail voter registration form. The commission had previously declined to implement part of Trump's March 2025 executive order requiring proof of citizenship on the national mail voter registration form — a requirement a federal judge later ruled exceeded presidential authority.

The White House said the president 'reserves the right to remove individuals that may not be totally aligned with the important task of securing America's elections.' The dismissals come after the Supreme Court ruled 6-3 last month that the president can fire members of independent agencies without cause. It is not yet clear whether Trump plans to nominate replacements or leave the seats vacant. The midterms are in November.
sections.whyItMatters: The EAC is now entirely empty four months before nationwide midterm elections. The commission's refusal to implement Trump's proof-of-citizenship order — later blocked by a court — was the apparent trigger. With the Supreme Court having expanded presidential removal power over independent agencies, the structural check that bipartisan commissions were designed to provide has been weakened. Whether Trump nominates replacements or leaves the EAC dormant will determine whether the federal government has any election-support infrastructure at all before November.

sections.heading: China Lands Reusable Rocket for First Time, Challenging US Dominance
sections.body:
China successfully landed a reusable rocket booster for the first time on Friday, state media reported. The Long March 10B lifted off from Hainan at 12:15 local time, and around six minutes after separating from the upper stage, the booster returned to Earth vertically and was recovered on a floating platform using 'landing hooks' that catch a net.

The Long March 10B can carry at least 16 metric tons to low-Earth orbit and has been compared to SpaceX's Falcon 9. Unlike the Falcon 9, which autonomously lands on a ground pad or drone ship, China's system uses a net-catching mechanism on a floating platform. China's previous attempt in February with the smaller Long March 10A achieved a controlled descent but only splashed down next to the recovery platform.

Shares in Chinese space firms jumped on the news — China Spacesat and China Satellite Communications each rose 10%, the daily limit. The milestone follows SpaceX's first Falcon 9 landing in 2015 and Blue Origin's New Glenn landing in November 2025, making China the third entity to demonstrate orbital-class reusable rocket recovery.
sections.whyItMatters: Reusable rocket technology is the key cost reducer for the commercial space industry — SpaceX's Falcon 9 now launches about 150 times a year with boosters reused dozens of times. China's successful landing narrows the gap with the US in a sector that determines access to satellite infrastructure, space-based intelligence, and eventual lunar and deep-space operations. The net-catching method is different from SpaceX's propulsive landing, and its reliability at scale remains to be proven, but the milestone itself signals that China's space program is moving from fast follower to direct competitor.

bottomLine: The US-Iran standoff over Hormuz has entered a new phase: talks will resume Saturday in Oman, but only under an American ultimatum that Iran publicly declare the strait open and stop firing on ships. Iran's private admission that the attacks were a 'mistake' gives both sides a face-saving path, but whether Tehran can — or will — deliver on a public commitment remains the open question. Meanwhile, two climate-driven crises are unfolding simultaneously: Spain's deadliest wildfire in decades has killed at least 12 with 23 missing, and Typhoon Bavi — one of the largest storms to hit East Asia in decades — is bearing down on Taiwan and China after killing 15 in the Philippines and on the heels of Typhoon Maysak, which killed 39. In Washington, Trump has emptied the federal election commission four months before midterms, leveraging a recent Supreme Court ruling expanding presidential removal power. And China's first reusable rocket landing marks a meaningful step in the strategic competition for access to space.
